beats.narration_text: Aloha — Liam, in for Bear. recitation-film: a public-domain spoken-word performance — a reading, a recitation — becomes a Vox-style film where the visual plane teaches the work's history and allusions while the listener hears the poem itself. The performance is the master clock. Marginalia that moves. Never a competing lecture.

props.runningText: plan → scholarship → align → run → stills → assemble...
output: Four margin laws: chips in breaths, ≤5 words, peaks=breathe
Scholarship gate: every chip gets a citation

beats.narration_text: Anatomy. One performance audio. One literary text. The visual plane carries three kinds of beats: illustrate — the edit argues by juxtaposition; teach — the margin cites something checkable; breathe — nothing labels, nothing competes. Four margin laws govern where chips land, how many words they carry, and which lines get silence. The chassis is inherited from the vox-explainer; the role axis and margin laws are new.

props.title: Performance + text → Vox marginalia film.
files: SKILL.md — margin laws, role axis, scholarship gate, karaoke-as-CC
_align.py — faster-whisper → words.json + breath map (run on Mac)
SHOTLIST.md — beat plan with roles, chips, plate descriptions
FACTCHECK.md — scholarship gate: citation per teach chip

beats.narration_text: Pipeline. Phase one: plan — write beats at breaths, assign roles, author chips under the margin laws, declare peak lines, run margin lint. Scholarship gate: every teach chip gets a citation in FACTCHECK dot md. Phase three: align — forced whisper on the performance audio produces words dot json and a breath map; spot-check five timestamps before anything keys to it. Phase four: the inherited compile machine builds the cut. Plates fill from generated stills or true-archive sources.

props.title: Plan → scholarship → align → run → plates.

phases.label: PLAN
phases.note: beats at breaths → roles → chips under margin laws → peak_lines declared → margin lint clean

phases.label: SCHOLARSHIP
phases.note: every teach chip sourced in FACTCHECK.md — gate: citations hold

phases.label: ALIGN
phases.note: _align.py (faster-whisper) → words.json + lines.json + breath map — Gate 0: spot-check 5

phases.label: RUN
phases.note: inherited compile.py machine pass; slates in unfilled slots; CC srt from lines.json

phases.label: PLATES
phases.note: generate stills (nano-banana) for period scenes; true-archive for cited objects

props.footerNote: Karaoke = CC track only — never a second cut. Burn-in only on explicit request.

beats.narration_text: Self-demo. Phase one is purely free — no audio, no video. Watch Claude plan four beats from Keats's Bright Star sonnet: peak line declared, margin laws checked, roles assigned, one teach chip with its citation.

props.runningText: lines → roles → chips → margin lint → scholarship...
output: Margin law checks + one teach chip cited

beats.narration_text: Line one: Bright star, would I were stedfast as thou art — role: breathe; peak line; no chip. Lines two and three: Not in lone splendour hung aloft the night, and watching with eternal lids apart — role: illustrate; plate: oil-dark sky, one star, period telescope. Line four: Like nature's patient sleepless Eremite — role: teach; chip in the breath: EREMITE, gloss, hermit, 1810 — four words. Citation: Oxford English Dictionary, eremite, first use 1810. Margin law checks: chip in breath — pass; four words, under five — pass; peak line has no chip — pass; teach count one of four beats, under the one-third cap — pass. Margin lint: clean.

props.title: Phase 1 — Keats 'Bright Star' (1819), lines 1–4
props.code:
LINE 1  "Bright star, would I were stedfast as thou art—"
role:   breathe  |  peak_line: TRUE  |  chip: NONE
note:   peak declared — margin must go silent

LINES 2–3  "Not in lone splendour hung aloft the night,
            And watching, with eternal lids apart,"
role:   illustrate  |  no chip
plate:  oil-dark sky, single star, period telescope,
        late Georgian medium; no lettering

LINE 4  "Like nature's patient, sleepless Eremite,"
role:   teach  |  chip in breath after line 4:
        EREMITE: hermit (1810)         [4 words ✓]
citation: OED, "eremite," first use 1810

MARGIN LAW CHECKS:
  ✓ chip in breath (not mid-line)
  ✓ ≤5 words (4)
  ✓ peak line = breathe, no chip
  ✓ teach cap: 1/4 beats < 1/3
LINT: CLEAN

beats.narration_text: Mechanism one. The performance is the master clock. Not an estimated duration, not a beat count — the spoken recording drives everything. Forced alignment with faster-whisper matches the known text to the audio millisecond by millisecond, produces a breath map, and locates every inter-line gap of at least three hundred fifty milliseconds. Those gaps are where the chips land. Nothing keys to alignment until the five-checkpoint spot-check passes.

props.heading: Forced alignment produces the breath map.
props.body: _align.py runs faster-whisper on the performance audio with the known text as the reference transcript. Output: words.json (word timestamps) + lines.json (line timestamps) + breath map (every inter-line gap ≥ 350ms). Chips land in those gaps — never mid-line. Gate 0: scrub five spot-checks before builders key to the alignment. Until then, uniform-line first pass, features flagged UNTRUSTED.
props.quote: The performance is the master clock.

beats.narration_text: Mechanism two. Two texts, one language channel. A vox explainer can talk and label at once because narration and the visual plane are one argument by one author. Here the listener's ears are full of the poem. Anything the plane says competes with it. So the plane never narrates: it illustrates by juxtaposition, it cites, or it breathes. That single constraint generates all four margin laws.

props.heading: The plane teaches without talking.
props.body: Three roles, one constraint: illustrate = plate stages the line, juxtaposition teaches silently. teach = margin cites something checkable (ALLUSION · PROVENANCE · GLOSS · STRUCTURE · ADAPTATION MARKER) — ≤5 words per chip. breathe = nothing. Interpretation is NEVER chipped — it lives in the edit (which plate, which cut). If a chip can't be cited, cut it or make it illustrate.

beats.narration_text: Mechanism three. Viewers came for the poem. That constraint caps teach beats at one third of the total, forbids two consecutive teach beats, and requires that the most charged lines — the peak lines declared at plan time — get breathe beats where the margin goes entirely silent. The film teaches, but the poem is always the foreground.

props.heading: Teach cap, no consecutive teach, peaks=breathe.
props.body: Margin Law 3: margin.peak_lines declared at plan time — those beats must be role=breathe, no chip ever shares the frame. Margin Law 4: teach ≤ 1/3 of beats; no two consecutive teach beats. The margin lint runner enforces both at plan gate — violations block the gate. Result: the teaching is real, but the poem is always louder.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. Claude, Reciting. recitation-film delivers literature marginalia built on the vox chassis: forced alignment locks the clock, role=breathe silences the peaks, and the scholarship gate ensures every teach chip is checkable. Viewers came for the poem.

artifactLines: performance audio → forced alignment → breath map (master clock)
role axis: illustrate | teach | breathe — chips only in breaths
scholarship gate: every teach chip cited in FACTCHECK.md
teach cap ≤ 1/3, peaks = breathe — poem stays foreground

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Run recitation-film on any public-domain spoken performance. Two questions before you start: which lines are the peaks — where must the margin go entirely silent? And have you sourced every teach chip in FACTCHECK dot md before the scholarship gate?

props.runningText: peaks declared? chips cited before scholarship gate?
output: Which lines are the peaks — where does the margin go silent?
Have you cited every teach chip in FACTCHECK.md?
Gate 0: spot-check 5 alignment timestamps before building

beats.narration_text: Claude, Reciting. I'm Liam, in for Bear.
